## [​](#sessions)Sessions

A session represents a complete interaction with an AI coding agent from start to finish. When you start Claude Code, or another supported agent, Entire automatically creates a new session.

### [​](#what’s-captured-in-a-session)What’s Captured in a Session

- Conversation transcript - The prompts you sent and responses from the AI

- Code changes - All file modifications made during the session

- Checkpoints - Save points you can rewind to

- Token usage - Input/output tokens, cache usage, and API call counts

- Metadata - Timestamps, agent type, and configuration

- Line Attribution - A calculation of how many lines agents and humans contributed to the session

All sessions are stored locally in your project folder as well as on GitHub when you push. To find your raw session data on GitHub, check out the [Sessions Branch](#sessions-branch) section below.

### [​](#nested-sessions)Nested Sessions

When an AI agent spawns sub-agents (like Claude Code’s Task tool), Entire captures these as nested sessions within the parent session, preserving the full hierarchy of interactions.

## [​](#checkpoints)Checkpoints

A checkpoint is a snapshot that you can rewind to—think of it as a “save point” in your work.
Checkpoints have a 12-character hex ID, like: `8a513f56ed70`, You’ll find Checkpoint IDs in the web application as well as in the CLI.

### [​](#types-of-checkpoints)Types of Checkpoints

Temporary Checkpoints
Created on shadow branches during active sessions. These contain the full state snapshot
with code and metadata, allowing you to rewind at any point during your session.  Shadow branches
are named `entire/<sessionID>-<worktreeID>`, but remain local.
Committed Checkpoints
When you make a Git commit, the checkpoint metadata is stored permanently on the
`entire/checkpoints/v1` branch, which gets synced with your upstream. The checkpoint ID is added
to your commit message as a trailer:Report inc

## [​](#getting-started)Getting Started

### [​](#sign-in-with-github)Sign In with GitHub

- Visit [entire.io](https://entire.io)

- Click Sign in and then Sign in with Github

- Authorize the Entire GitHub App

- Your repositories will be automatically synced

Entire will never modify your code.

### [​](#connect-your-repositories)Connect Your Repositories

After signing in, you’ll see your GitHub repositories. Repositories with Entire enabled will show their sessions and checkpoints.

## [​](#navigation)Navigation

### [​](#global-navigation)Global Navigation

- Dashboard - Overview of your AI coding activity

- Repositories - Browse all your connected repositories

- Profile - Manage your account settings

### [​](#repository-navigation)Repository Navigation

Within a repository:

- Overview - Repository summary and recent activity

- Checkpoints - Browse checkpoints by branch

- Sessions - All sessions within a Checkpoint

## [​](#features)Features

## Dashboard
View your AI coding activity, recent sessions, and aggregate metrics
## Repositories
Browse connected repositories and manage settings
## Sessions
View session transcripts, tool calls, and line attribution
## Checkpoints
Browse checkpoints by branch and view code changes

## [​](#data-privacy)Data Privacy

- Session data is stored securely and encrypted in Entire.io. Note that your session data is stored in a branch on your repository.

- Only you and authorized team members can access your sessions

- You can delete sessions at any time

- Entire.io attempts to anonymize sensitive data such as API tokens in the session transcript

- See our [Privacy Policy](https://entire.io/privacy) for full details

-  Linux

brew install entireio/tap/entire

```

Verify the installation:
Report incorrect codeCopyAsk AI

entire version

```

## [​](#enable-entire-in-your-repository)Enable Entire in Your Repository

Navigate to any Git repository and enable Entire:
Report incorrect codeCopyAsk AI
```
cd your-project
entire enable

```

This installs Git hooks that automatically capture checkpoints as you work with AI agents.
Entire captures checkpoints when you make commits, without adding extra commits to your branch. See [Checkpoints](/cli/checkpoints) for details.

## [​](#start-a-coding-session)Start a Coding Session

Now, use your AI coding assistant as you normally would:
Report incorrect codeCopyAsk AI
```
# Example with Claude Code
claude

# Example with Gemini CLI
gemini

# Example with OpenCode
opencode

# Example with Factory Droid
droid

```

At this point you have Entire enabled in your repo! From now on, when your agent makes changes and you commit them, your commit is automatically linked to a checkpoint containing the full session context. On your next push to remote, your checkpoints will be pushed as well.

[Entire home page](/)Search...⌘KAsk AISearch...NavigationEntire.ioSessions[Documentation](/introduction)[Documentation](/introduction)Sessions are complete records of your AI coding interactions. The Entire web app provides tools to browse, search, and understand your session history.

## [​](#checkpoints-and-sessions)Checkpoints and Sessions

Checkpoints and sessions have a many-to-many relationship depending on the developer’s workflow. Here are the scenarios:

- Single checkpoint, single session - The most common workflow. You ask Claude Code to implement a feature, it does it and then you create a commit and push it.

- Single checkpoint, multiple sessions - A developer might have multiple Claude Code sessions working on a single feature and once done, the developer pushes a single checkpoint.

- Multiple checkpoints, single session - A developer might have a single session running in a terminal while creating multiple commits throughout that session and thereby creating multiple checkpoints. Entire will match up these checkpoints to the single session.

- Multiple checkpoints, multiple sessions - A developer might have multiple terminals with multiple sessions and checkpoints.

## [​](#viewing-sessions)Viewing Sessions

Click on a checkpoint to view it’s sessions. Each session card shows:

- Date and time - When the session occurred

- Duration - How long the session lasted

- Steps - How many steps the conversation took between the user and agent

- Tool Calls - How many tools were called

- Token usage - Total tokens consumed

Attribution - The percentage breakdown between AI Agents and Humans who contributed to the code
See a breakdown of who wrote the code: agent vs. human.

- Your prompts - What you asked the AI

- AI responses - What the agent replied

Conversation transcripts help during code review—reviewers can see the reasoning behind changes.

## Entire.io - Comprehensive Overview

### 1. What is Entire.io?

Entire.io is a developer platform that integrates with your Git workflow to automatically capture and manage AI coding agent sessions. It's designed to unify code with its context and reasoning by introducing **Checkpoints** - a new primitive that captures agent context as first-class, versioned data in Git.

When you commit code generated by an AI agent (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenCode, Factory Droid, or Copilot CLI), Entire automatically captures the complete session context alongside the commit. This context becomes searchable, shareable, and permanent.

### 2. Data Model: Key Entities & Relationships

The platform has several interconnected core entities:

#### **Sessions**
A session represents a complete interaction with an AI coding agent from start to finish. When you start an AI coding assistant, Entire automatically creates a new session.

**What's Captured in a Session:**
- Conversation transcript (prompts and AI responses)
- Code changes (all file modifications)
- Checkpoints (save points within the session)
- Token usage (input/output tokens, cache usage, API call counts)
- Metadata (timestamps, agent type, configuration)
- Line attribution (breakdown of human vs. AI contributions)

**Nested Sessions:** When an AI agent spawns sub-agents (e.g., Claude Code's Task tool), Entire captures these as nested sessions within the parent session, preserving the full hierarchy of interactions.

#### **Checkpoints**
A checkpoint is a snapshot/save point that you can rewind to during your work. Each checkpoint has a unique 12-character hex ID (e.g., `8a513f56ed70`).

**Types of Checkpoints:**
1. **Temporary Checkpoints** - Created on shadow branches during active sessions. These contain the full state snapshot with code and metadata, allowing you to rewind at any point. Shadow branches are named `entire/<sessionID>-<worktreeID>` and remain local.

2. **Committed Checkpoints** - When you make a Git commit, the checkpoint metadata is stored permanently on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch, which syncs with your remote. The checkpoint ID is added to your commit message as a trailer:
   ```
   Add user authentication
   
   Entire-Checkpoint: a3b2c4d5e6f7
   ```

**Checkpoint-Session Relationships:**
- Single checkpoint, single session (most common)
- Single checkpoint, multiple sessions (multiple agent interactions on one feature)
- Multiple checkpoints, single session (multiple commits in one session)
- Multiple checkpoints, multiple sessions (complex multi-terminal workflows)

#### **Git Storage & Branches**
- **Shadow Branches:** `entire/<sessionID>-<worktreeID>` - temporary, local branches for active sessions
- **Checkpoints Branch:** `entire/checkpoints/v1` - permanent branch storing metadata (JSON files only, not code) for all completed sessions and checkpoints, synced with upstream
- **Sessions Branch:** Raw session data stored both locally and on GitHub when pushed

#### **Repositories**
Connected GitHub repositories that have Entire enabled. The web application syncs repositories automatically after GitHub sign-in.

#### **Line Attribution**
A calculated metric showing how many lines of code were contributed by agents vs. humans, accounting for interleaved human edits and agent changes across multiple checkpoints.

### 3. How It Works - Main Features & Workflow

#### **Installation & Setup**
1. Install Entire CLI (via Homebrew, curl script, or Go)
2. Enable Entire in any Git repository: `entire enable`
3. This installs Git hooks that automatically capture checkpoints on commit
4. Sign in to entire.io with GitHub for web access

#### **Capture Strategy**
- Checkpoints are created automatically on Git commits (without adding extra commits)
- Temporary checkpoints allow rewinding during active sessions
- Committed checkpoints preserve session context permanently in Git
- Token usage is tracked per checkpoint (input tokens, output tokens, cache creation, cache reads, API calls)

#### **Token Usage Tracking**
Entire tracks detailed token metrics:
- Input tokens sent to the AI
- Output tokens received from the AI
- Cache creation tokens
- Cache reads from cache
- API call counts
- Helps understand AI usage patterns and costs

#### **Web Application Features**

**Dashboard:**
- Recent checkpoints across all repositories
- Activity feed and timeline
- Aggregate statistics on AI coding sessions
- Quick navigation to checkpoints, repositories, and profile

**Repositories:**
- Browse connected GitHub repositories
- View repository summary and recent activity
- Manage repository settings
- Filter checkpoints by branch

**Checkpoints View:**
- Browse all checkpoints chronologically by branch
- View checkpoint ID, timestamp, owner, agent type
- See file diffs, session count, token count
- Click to view full code changes and session details

**Sessions View:**
- View complete session transcripts
- See tool calls and arguments
- Analyze token usage breakdown
- Review line attribution (agent vs. human)
- File tab view showing all modified files with side-by-side or unified diffs
- Helps during code review by showing reasoning behind changes

#### **Data Privacy & Security**
- Session data encrypted and stored securely on Entire.io
- Only authorized team members can access sessions
- Sessions can be deleted at any time
- Sensitive data (API tokens) anonymized in transcripts
- Session data also stored in Git branch on repository

#### **Supported Integrations**
- Claude Code (primary)
- Gemini CLI (preview)
- OpenCode (preview)
- Factory Droid (preview)
- Copilot CLI (preview)
- Cursor

### 4. Key Differentiators

The platform solves a critical gap in AI-assisted development:
- **Permanent context capture** - Sessions don't disappear when the terminal closes
- **Searchable reasoning** - Understand not just what changed, but why
- **Git-native** - Leverages existing Git workflow without disruption
- **First-class versioning** - Checkpoints treated as versioned entities like code
- **Code review enhancement** - Reviewers can see agent reasoning and prompts
- **Multi-agent support** - Handles nested and complex agent interactions

1→# Agentic Coding Talk — ATLS 4214
     2→
     3→Materials for a guest lecture on agentic coding for the University of Colorado ATLAS program, [ATLS 4214: Big Data Architecture](https://www.colorado.edu/atlas/academics/undergraduate/bs-ctd-focus-electives).
     4→
     5→## What's here
     6→
     7→This repo contains two things built concurrently:
     8→
     9→- **`slides/`** — the slide deck for the talk
    10→- **`project/`** — a toy log analytics pipeline, used as a live demo
    11→
    12→The project is built *using* the techniques described in the talk: a top-down planning loop paired with bottom-up implementation driven by an AI coding agent.
    13→
    14→## The toy project
    15→
    16→`project/` is a small CLI tool called `logpipe` that ingests web server logs and lets you query aggregated metrics — error rates, request counts, latency percentiles.
    17→
    18→It's intentionally simple, but built the way a real project should be: **black-box integration tests written first**, implementation second. The tests are the contract; the agent's job is to make them pass.
    19→
    20→See [`project/README.md`](project/README.md) for setup and usage instructions.
    21→
    22→## The talk
    23→
    24→The core argument: agentic coding tools don't change *what* good software looks like — clear interfaces, fast feedback loops, readable code. They change *how fast* you can get there, and *where* you spend your attention.
    25→
    26→Topics covered:
    27→
    28→1. What "agentic coding" actually means (vs. autocomplete)
    29→2. Top-down planning with an AI agent
    30→3. Bottom-up implementation driven by tests
    31→4. Where agents excel and where they still need you
    32→5. Live demo: building `logpipe` from scratch
    33→
    34→## Repo layout
    35→
    36→```
    37→├── README.md          # you are here
    38→├── AGENT.md           # context for the AI coding agent working in this repo
    39→├── slides/            # slide deck (Marp — run `npm run dev` to preview)
    40→└── project/           # logpipe — the toy demo project
    41→```
    42→

1→# logpipe
     2→
     3→A log analytics CLI built as a demo for ATLS 4214.
     4→
     5→## Setup
     6→
     7→Requires Python 3.11+ and [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).
     8→
     9→```bash
    10→cd project
    11→uv sync
    12→```
    13→
    14→## Usage
    15→
    16→### Ingest logs
    17→
    18→Parse log lines and echo them as JSON:
    19→
    20→```bash
    21→uv run logpipe ingest access.log
    22→uv run logpipe ingest -   # read from stdin
    23→```
    24→
    25→Output is one JSON object per valid line:
    26→
    27→```json
    28→{"host": "127.0.0.1", "user": "-", "ts": 971211336, "method": "GET", "path": "/index.html", "status": 200, "bytes": 1234, "response_time": 0.021}
    29→```
    30→
    31→Malformed lines are silently skipped.
    32→
    33→### Query logs
    34→
    35→Filter log records with a WHERE expression:
    36→
    37→```bash
    38→uv run logpipe query "status >= 400" access.log
    39→uv run logpipe query "status >= 400" -     # read from stdin
    40→```
    41→
    42→Expressions support `=`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` (numeric/string), and `~` (substring):
    43→
    44→```bash
    45→uv run logpipe query "status >= 400 AND method = POST" access.log
    46→uv run logpipe query "status = 200 OR status = 201" access.log
    47→uv run logpipe query "path ~ /api" access.log
    48→uv run logpipe query "response_time > 1.0" access.log
    49→```
    50→
    51→Filterable fields: `host`, `user`, `ts`, `method`, `path`, `status`, `bytes`, `response_time`.
    52→
    53→Output is one JSON object per matching line. Non-matching lines produce no output.
    54→
    55→## Run tests
    56→
    57→```bash
    58→uv run pytest
    59→```
    60→
    61→## Log format
    62→
    63→Expects Extended Combined Log Format — standard Apache/nginx access logs with an
    64→appended `response_time` field (seconds):
    65→
    66→```
    67→127.0.0.1 - frank [10/Oct/2000:13:55:36 -0700] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1234 0.021
    68→```

1→# logpipe — Query Pipeline Architecture
     2→
     3→This document describes the full intended query pipeline architecture. Individual planning sessions implement one stage at a time; consult this doc for context on how each piece fits.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## SQL-Style Execution Order
     8→
     9→logpipe's `query` command follows SQL's logical processing order:
    10→
    11→```
    12→FROM  →  WHERE  →  WINDOW  →  AGGREGATE  →  SELECT  →  HAVING
    13→```
    14→
    15→| Stage | CLI | Status | Description |
    16→|-------|-----|--------|-------------|
    17→| FROM | positional `source` arg | Implemented (via `ingest`) | Log file path or `-` for stdin |
    18→| WHERE | positional `expr` arg | Planned (current work) | Filter expression string, e.g. `"status >= 400 AND method = POST"` |
    19→| WINDOW | `--window` | Future | Partition stream into time-based buckets before aggregation |
    20→| AGGREGATE | `--group-by`, aggregate functions in SELECT | Future | Group records and compute aggregates (within each window if WINDOW is active) |
    21→| SELECT | `--select` | Future | Project fields; define computed / aggregated columns |
    22→| HAVING | `--having` | Future | Filter on projected / aggregated values |
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## Pipeline Abstraction
    27→
    28→Each stage implements the `Stage` protocol and is composed into a `Pipeline`:
    29→
    30→```python
    31→class Stage(Protocol):
    32→    def process(self, records: Iterable[Any]) -> Iterable[Any]: ...
    33→
    34→class Pipeline:
    35→    def run(self, source: Iterable[Any]) -> Iterable[Any]: ...
    36→```
    37→
    38→Stages are streamed — records flow through lazily where possible. Stages that require full materialization (e.g. AGGREGATE) consume the stream and emit a new one.
    39→
    40→```
    41→Source (lazy) → WHERE (lazy) → WINDOW (materializes per bucket) → AGGREGATE (materializes per group) → SELECT (lazy) → HAVING (lazy) → output
    42→```
    43→
    44→---
    45→
    46→## Stage Designs
    47→
    48→### FROM (implicit)
    49→
    50→Already implemented by `ingest`. The `query` command re-uses `parse_line()` from `logpipe/parser.py` to produce a stream of `LogRecord` objects from a file or stdin.
    51→
    52→---
    53→
    54→### WHERE
    55→
    56→Filters the `LogRecord` stream with boolean predicates.
    57→
    58→**Expression grammar:**
    59→```
    60→expr   := clause (("AND" | "OR") clause)*
    61→clause := FIELD OP VALUE
    62→FIELD  := host | user | ts | method | path | status | bytes | response_time
    63→OP     := "=" | "!=" | "<" | ">" | "<=" | ">=" | "~"
    64→VALUE  := bare word | quoted string | number
    65→```
    66→
    67→Multiple `--where` flags are AND-ed at the top level.
    68→
    69→**Key types:**
    70→```python
    71→Predicate = Callable[[LogRecord], bool]
    72→
    73→class WhereStage:
    74→    predicate: Predicate
    75→    def process(self, records: Iterable[LogRecord]) -> Iterable[LogRecord]: ...
    76→```
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→### WINDOW
    81→
    82→Partitions the filtered `LogRecord` stream into time-based buckets. AGGREGATE then runs independently within each bucket, enabling time-series analysis.
    83→
    84→This is stream-processing semantics (closer to Flink/Spark Structured Streaming than SQL window functions).
    85→
    86→**Intended CLI shape:**
    87→```bash
    88→logpipe query "status >= 400" \
    89→        --window 5m \
    90→        --group-by host \
    91→        --select "host, COUNT(*) as errors" \
    92→        access.log
    93→```
    94→
    95→**Window types (planned):** tumbling (non-overlapping fixed-size buckets), sliding (overlapping), session (gap-based)
    96→
    97→**Key types (sketch):**
    98→```python
    99→from datetime import timedelta
   100→from enum import Enum
   101→
   102→class WindowType(Enum):
   103→    TUMBLING = "tumbling"
   104→    SLIDING  = "sliding"
   105→    SESSION  = "session"
   106→
   107→@dataclass
   108→class WindowSpec:
   109→    type: WindowType
   110→    size: timedelta           # bucket duration
   111→    slide: timedelta | None   # for sliding windows; None → same as size
   112→
   113→class WindowStage:
   114→    spec: WindowSpec
   115→    def process(self, records: Iterable[LogRecord]) -> Iterable[tuple[int, Iterable[LogRecord]]]:
   116→        # yields (bucket_start_ts, records_in_bucket)
   117→        ...
   118→```
   119→
   120→WINDOW materializes records per bucket (must see all records in a window before emitting). For tumbling windows on ordered logs this is bounded; for unordered logs a watermark/grace period is needed (future concern).
   121→
   122→---
   123→
   124→### AGGREGATE
   125→
   126→Groups records by one or more fields and computes aggregate functions over each group.
   127→
   128→**Intended CLI shape:**
   129→```bash
   130→logpipe query --where "status >= 400" \
   131→              --group-by host \
   132→              --select "host, COUNT(*) as errors, AVG(response_time) as avg_rt" \
   133→              access.log
   134→```
   135→
   136→**Aggregate functions (planned):** `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `P50`, `P95`, `P99`
   137→
   138→**Key types (sketch):**
   139→```python
   140→@dataclass
   141→class GroupKey:
   142→    fields: list[str]
   143→
   144→@dataclass
   145→class AggregateExpr:
   146→    fn: str          # "COUNT", "AVG", etc.
   147→    field: str | None  # None for COUNT(*)
   148→    alias: str
   149→
   150→class AggregateStage:
   151→    group_key: GroupKey
   152→    aggregates: list[AggregateExpr]
   153→    def process(self, records: Iterable[LogRecord]) -> Iterable[dict]: ...
   154→```
   155→
   156→Note: AggregateStage materializes the full stream into memory (one dict per group), then emits the group rows. Output is `dict` not `LogRecord` from this point forward.
   157→
   158→---
   159→
   160→### SELECT
   161→
   162→Projects specific fields and computed values from each row. After AGGREGATE, rows are dicts; SELECT shapes what appears in the final output.
   163→
   164→**Intended CLI shape:**
   165→```bash
   166→--select "host, status, response_time"
   167→--select "host, COUNT(*) as count"   # (with --group-by)
   168→```
   169→
   170→Without AGGREGATE, SELECT simply picks fields from `LogRecord`. With AGGREGATE, it names which computed columns to emit.
   171→
   172→**Key types (sketch):**
   173→```python
   174→@dataclass
   175→class SelectExpr:
   176→    field: str
   177→    alias: str | None
   178→
   179→class SelectStage:
   180→    exprs: list[SelectExpr]
   181→    def process(self, records: Iterable[dict]) -> Iterable[dict]: ...
   182→```
   183→
   184→---
   185→
   186→### HAVING
   187→
   188→Filters rows after AGGREGATE + SELECT, using the same expression syntax as WHERE but operating on the projected dict keys (including aggregate aliases).
   189→
   190→```bash
   191→logpipe query --group-by host \
   192→              --select "host, COUNT(*) as errors" \
   193→              --having "errors > 10" \
   194→              access.log
   195→```
   196→
   197→**Key types (sketch):**
   198→```python
   199→class HavingStage:
   200→    predicate: Callable[[dict], bool]
   201→    def process(self, records: Iterable[dict]) -> Iterable[dict]: ...
   202→```
   203→
   204→The predicate parser for HAVING is similar to WHERE's, but operates on `dict` keys rather than `LogRecord` attributes.
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## Full Data Flow
   209→
   210→```mermaid
   211→flowchart LR
   212→    SRC["FROM\nfile / stdin"] -->|raw lines| P["parse_line()"]
   213→    P -->|LogRecord| W["WHERE\nWhereStage"]
   214→    W -->|LogRecord| WI["WINDOW\nWindowStage"]
   215→    WI -->|bucketed LogRecords| AG["AGGREGATE\nAggregateStage"]
   216→    AG -->|dict per group| SEL["SELECT\nSelectStage"]
   217→    SEL -->|projected dict| HAV["HAVING\nHavingStage"]
   218→    HAV -->|dict| OUT["JSON stdout"]
   219→
   220→    style W fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
   221→    style WI fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   222→    style AG fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   223→    style SEL fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   224→    style HAV fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   225→```
   226→
   227→When a stage is omitted from the CLI invocation, it is a no-op passthrough (or skipped entirely in the pipeline construction).
   228→
   229→---
   230→
   231→## Module Layout (target state)
   232→
   233→```
   234→logpipe/
   235→  parser.py        LogRecord, parse_line()              (done)
   236→  query.py         Stage, Pipeline, WhereStage,         (in progress)
   237→                   AggregateStage, SelectStage,
   238→                   HavingStage, expression parsers
   239→  cli.py           ingest + query commands               (extending)
   240→```
   241→
   242→---
   243→
   244→## Non-Goals
   245→
   246→- SQL string parsing (no `SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE` syntax — each clause is a separate CLI flag)
   247→- Joins across multiple sources
   248→- Persistent storage or indexing
   249→- Windowing / time-bucketing (may be added as a variant of AGGREGATE)
   250→

# Agentic Coding
### How AI agents change the way we build software

ATLS 4214 — Big Data Architecture

---

# What is agentic coding?

Not autocomplete. Not a search engine.

An agent that can **read your codebase**, **write files**, **run tests**, and **iterate** — with you in the loop.

> "LLM tooling can be a multiplier for your effectiveness — you just have to make sure you are bringing a value greater than 1 to multiply by."

---

# Theme 1: LLMs are a tool

- They shift the engineer's role toward **design**, **requirements**, and **testing**
- Tell it *what* you want, not *how* to do it
  - Describe the outcome, constraints, and context
  - Leave implementation to the tool unless the "how" is critical
- Sweating the details — naming, internal APIs, structure — creates a semantically compact language that both you *and* the LLM can reason in effectively

---

# Fundamentals still matter

- Wrestling with problems builds understanding — that understanding is what lets you direct the tool well
- *"These tools are great at removing accidental friction, but not all friction is bad."*
- Open question: how do we ensure new engineers still build foundational experience?

---

# Theme 2: Show your work

LLMs strongly incentivize **solo work** — resist this.

- Pairing builds shared context and surfaces what you didn't know you didn't know
- Code review builds collective ownership
- The goal: a team with a **shared reality** of what they own and operate

Process and tooling should shift focus: not just *generating* artifacts, but running them through that shared reality.

---

# Workflows that capture intent

Concrete practices:

- **Generate artifacts to react to** — a plan or writeup sidesteps the blank page problem; editing is easier than creating from nothing
- **Stage changes between chunks** — the working diff always shows what the agent is currently doing
- **Review before it lands** — never be surprised by a commit

---

# Top-down: plan → implement

The `plan` → `process-feedback` → `implement` loop:

- Write a high-level plan *before* touching code — iterate your thinking cheaply
- Share the plan for early feedback **before any code exists**
- Plan files live in the repo: future you (and future LLM sessions) can read them
- Forces you to articulate *what* and *why*, not just let the agent decide *how*

---

# Bottom-up: entire.io

[entire.io](https://entire.io) records your coding session as it happens:

- Captures the *actual* sequence of decisions — not a cleaned-up post-hoc summary
- Complements the plan: **the plan is the intent, the recording is the reality**
- Useful for your own review, async teammates, and future LLM sessions

Top-down + bottom-up = a workflow where the work is **legible to others**

---

# Theme 3: Give the agent a feedback loop

LLMs do well with **objective measures of correctness** they can react to.

- A running test suite is the tightest feedback loop you can give an agent
- Integration tests > unit tests
  - Harder to "fake"
  - Cast a wider net across the system

---

# Black-box integration tests

Virtuous cycle:

```
write seed tests → agent makes them pass
               ↓
        agent writes more tests
               ↓
      better coverage + honest signal
```

For `logpipe`: feed in raw log lines, assert on query output — **no mocking internals**.

---

# This talk, built with these tools

This presentation and `logpipe` were built using the exact workflows just described:

- **Top-down**: `plan` → `process-feedback` → `implement` — plan files are in `_plans/` in this repo
- **Bottom-up**: entire.io recorded the sessions — you can watch how the work unfolded

The talk is its own demo. You don't have to take my word for any of this.

---

---

# What is agentic coding?

Not autocomplete. Not a search engine.

An agent that can **read your codebase**, **write files**, **run tests**, and **iterate** — with you in the loop.

> "LLM tooling can be a multiplier for your effectiveness — you just have to make sure you are bringing a value greater than 1 to multiply by."

---

# Theme 1: LLMs are a tool

- They shift the engineer's role toward **design**, **requirements**, and **testing**
- Tell it *what* you want, not *how* to do it
  - Describe the outcome, constraints, and context
  - Leave implementation to the tool unless the "how" is critical
- Sweating the details — naming, internal APIs, structure — creates a semantically compact language that both you *and* the LLM can reason in effectively

---

# Fundamentals still matter

- Wrestling with problems builds understanding — that understanding is what lets you direct the tool well
- *"These tools are great at removing accidental friction, but not all friction is bad."*
- Open question: how do we ensure new engineers still build foundational experience?

---

# Theme 2: Show your work

LLMs strongly incentivize **solo work** — resist this.

- Pairing builds shared context and surfaces what you didn't know you didn't know
- Code review builds collective ownership
- The goal: a team with a **shared reality** of what they own and operate

Process and tooling should shift focus: not just *generating* artifacts, but running them through that shared reality.

---

# Workflows that capture intent

Concrete practices:

- **Generate artifacts to react to** — a plan or writeup sidesteps the blank page problem; editing is easier than creating from nothing
- **Stage changes between chunks** — the working diff always shows what the agent is currently doing
- **Review before it lands** — never be surprised by a commit

---

# Top-down: plan → implement

The `plan` → `process-feedback` → `implement` loop:

- Write a high-level plan *before* touching code — iterate your thinking cheaply
- Share the plan for early feedback **before any code exists**
- Plan files live in the repo: future you (and future LLM sessions) can read them
- Forces you to articulate *what* and *why*, not just let the agent decide *how*

---

# Bottom-up: entire.io

[entire.io](https://entire.io) records your coding session as it happens:

- Captures the *actual* sequence of decisions — not a cleaned-up post-hoc summary
- Complements the plan: **the plan is the intent, the recording is the reality**
- Useful for your own review, async teammates, and future LLM sessions

Top-down + bottom-up = a workflow where the work is **legible to others**

---

# Theme 3: Give the agent a feedback loop

LLMs do well with **objective measures of correctness** they can react to.

- A running test suite is the tightest feedback loop you can give an agent
- Integration tests > unit tests
  - Harder to "fake"
  - Cast a wider net across the system

---

# Black-box integration tests

Virtuous cycle:

```
write seed tests → agent makes them pass
               ↓
        agent writes more tests
               ↓
      better coverage + honest signal
```

For `logpipe`: feed in raw log lines, assert on query output — **no mocking internals**.

---

# This talk, built with these tools

This presentation and `logpipe` were built using the exact workflows just described:

- **Top-down**: `plan` → `process-feedback` → `implement` — plan files are in `_plans/` in this repo
- **Bottom-up**: entire.io recorded the sessions — you can watch how the work unfolded

The talk is its own demo. You don't have to take my word for any of this.

---

    41→        status=int(d["status"]),

### def parse_and(self) -> Predicate:

def parse_and(self) -> Predicate:
        left = self.parse_clause()
        while self.peek() and self.peek().upper() == "AND":  # type: ignore[union-attr]
            self.consume()
            right = self.parse_clause()
            left = _and(left, right)
        return left

### def parse_clause(self) -> Predicate:

def parse_clause(self) -> Predicate:
        field = self.consume("field name")
        if field.upper() in ("AND", "OR"):
            raise ValueError(
                f"Expected field name, got keyword {field!r} in: {self.expr!r}"
            )
        if field not in VALID_FIELDS:
            raise ValueError(
                f"Unknown field {field!r}. Valid fields: {sorted(VALID_FIELDS)}"
            )
        op = self.consume("operator")
        if op not in VALID_OPS:
            raise ValueError(
                f"Unknown operator {op!r}. Valid operators: {sorted(VALID_OPS)}"
            )
        value_tok = self.consume("value")
        fe = FilterExpr(field=field, op=op, value=_coerce(field, value_tok))  # type: ignore[arg-type]
        return _make_predicate(fe)

    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→### SQL-Style Pipeline
    20→
    21→The full query pipeline follows SQL's logical processing order:
    22→
    23→```
    24→FROM      → implicit: the source argument (file or stdin)
    25→WHERE     → filter raw LogRecords               ← THIS PLAN
    26→WINDOW    → partition stream into time buckets   (future)
    27→AGGREGATE → group-by + aggregate functions       (future)
    28→SELECT    → project fields / computed values     (future)
    29→HAVING    → filter on projected/aggregated cols  (future)
    30→```
    31→
    32→**Where windowing fits:** WINDOW sits between WHERE and AGGREGATE. It partitions the filtered record stream into time-based buckets (e.g. 5-minute tumbling windows), and AGGREGATE then runs independently within each bucket. This is distinct from SQL window functions — it's closer to stream-processing semantics (think Flink/Spark Structured Streaming).
    33→
    34→Each stage is implemented as a `Stage` object in a `Pipeline`. Future plans add stages; this plan adds `WhereStage`.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→### CLI Interface
    39→
    40→The `query` command takes the WHERE expression as a positional string argument, followed by the source. This keeps the interface simple as expressions grow more complex — no flag juggling.
    41→
    42→```bash
    43→# Filter from a log file
    44→logpipe query "status >= 400" access.log
    45→
    46→# AND / OR inside the expression
    47→logpipe query "status >= 400 AND method = POST" access.log
    48→logpipe query "status = 200 OR status = 201" access.log
    49→
    50→# Stdin
    51→logpipe query "response_time > 1.0" -
    52→```
    53→
    54→**Supported operators:**
    55→
    56→| Op | Meaning |
    57→|----|---------|
    58→| `=` | equality |
    59→| `!=` | inequality |
    60→| `<` `>` `<=` `>=` | numeric / lexicographic comparison |
    61→| `~` | substring match (string fields) |
    62→
    63→**Filterable fields** (all fields of `LogRecord`):
    64→`host`, `user`, `ts`, `method`, `path`, `status`, `bytes`, `response_time`
    65→
    66→---
    67→
    68→### Module Changes
    69→
    70→| File | Change |
    71→|------|--------|
    72→| `logpipe/query.py` | **New.** Filter expression parser, `Op` type, `FilterExpr`, `Predicate`, `WhereStage`, `Pipeline`. |
    73→| `logpipe/cli.py` | Add `query` command; wire positional `expr` arg through pipeline. |
    74→| `tests/test_query.py` | **New.** Black-box tests for `logpipe query`. |
    75→| `project/README.md` | Add `query` command usage section. |
    76→| `project/ARCHITECTURE.md` | **New.** Forward-looking query pipeline design doc. |
    77→
    78→No changes to `parser.py` (LogRecord stays as-is).
    79→
    80→---
    81→
    82→### Data / UX Flow
    83→
    84→```mermaid
    85→flowchart LR
    86→    A["FROM\nfile or stdin"] -->|raw lines| B["parse_line()\nlogpipe/parser.py"]
    87→    B -->|LogRecord stream| C["WHERE\nWhereStage"]
    88→    C -->|matching LogRecords| D["JSON output\nstdout"]
    89→
    90→    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    91→```
    92→
    93→**Full future pipeline (for context — not in this plan):**
    94→
    95→```mermaid
    96→flowchart LR
    97→    A["FROM"] --> B["parse"]
    98→    B --> C["WHERE"]
    99→    C --> D["WINDOW\n(future)"]
   100→    D --> E["AGGREGATE\n(future)"]
   101→    E --> F["SELECT\n(future)"]
   102→    F --> G["HAVING\n(future)"]
   103→    G --> H["output"]
   104→
   105→    style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
   106→    style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   107→    style E fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   108→    style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   109→    style G fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
   110→```
   111→
   112→---
   113→
   114→### Core Domain Objects (`logpipe/query.py`)
   115→
   116→```python
   117→from typing import Callable, Iterable, Any, Literal, Protocol
   118→from dataclasses import dataclass
   119→from logpipe.parser import LogRecord
   120→
   121→# ------------------------------------------------------------------
   122→# Strong type for comparison operators
   123→# ------------------------------------------------------------------
   124→Op = Literal["=", "!=", "<", ">", "<=", ">=", "~"]
   125→
   126→# ------------------------------------------------------------------
   127→# Predicate: a function from LogRecord → bool
   128→# ------------------------------------------------------------------
   129→Predicate = Callable[[LogRecord], bool]
   130→
   131→
   132→@dataclass
   133→class FilterExpr:
   134→    """A single comparison: field op value."""
   135→    field: str   # e.g. "status"
   136→    op: Op       # one of the 7 operators — not an arbitrary string
   137→    value: Any   # coerced to field's native type
   138→
   139→
   140→def parse_predicate(expr: str) -> Predicate:
   141→    """
   142→    Parse a WHERE expression string into a callable predicate.
   143→    Supports AND / OR combinators (no parentheses in v1).
   144→
   145→    Examples:
   146→        "status >= 400"
   147→        "method = GET OR method = POST"
   148→        "response_time > 1.0"
   149→    """
   150→    ...
   151→
   152→
   153→# ------------------------------------------------------------------
   154→# Pipeline stage abstraction (extensibility hook)
   155→# ------------------------------------------------------------------
   156→class Stage(Protocol):
   157→    def process(self, records: Iterable[Any]) -> Iterable[Any]: ...
   158→
   159→
   160→@dataclass
   161→class WhereStage:
   162→    predicate: Predicate
   163→
   164→    def process(self, records: Iterable[LogRecord]) -> Iterable[LogRecord]:
   165→        return (r for r in records if self.predicate(r))
   166→
   167→
   168→class Pipeline:
   169→    def __init__(self, stages: list[Stage]) -> None:
   170→        self.stages = stages
   171→
   172→    def run(self, source: Iterable[Any]) -> Iterable[Any]:
   173→        stream = source
   174→        for stage in self.stages:
   175→            stream = stage.process(stream)
   176→        return stream
   177→```
   178→
   179→---
   180→
   181→### CLI addition (`logpipe/cli.py`)
   182→
   183→```python
   184→@app.command()
   185→def query(
   186→    expr: Annotated[str, typer.Argument(help="Filter expression, e.g. 'status >= 400 AND method = POST'")],
   187→    source: Annotated[str, typer.Argument(help="Log file path, or - for stdin")],
   188→):
   189→    """Query log records with a filter expression."""
   190→    pipeline = Pipeline([WhereStage(parse_predicate(expr))])
   191→
   192→    fh = sys.stdin if source == "-" else open(source)
   193→    try:
   194→        raw_records = (parse_line(line) for line in fh)
   195→        valid_records = (r for r in raw_records if r is not None)
   196→        for record in pipeline.run(valid_records):
   197→            print(json.dumps(asdict(record)))
   198→    finally:
   199→        if fh is not sys.stdin:
   200→            fh.close()
   201→```
   202→
   203→---
   204→
   205→### Filter Expression Parser Design
   206→
   207→A hand-rolled tokenizer + parser (no third-party deps). Grammar:
   208→
   209→```
   210→expr   := clause (("AND" | "OR") clause)*
   211→clause := FIELD OP VALUE
   212→FIELD  := host | user | ts | method | path | status | bytes | response_time
   213→OP     := "=" | "!=" | "<" | ">" | "<=" | ">=" | "~"
   214→VALUE  := bare word, quoted string, or number
   215→```
   216→
   217→Notes:
   218→- `AND` binds tighter than `OR` (standard boolean precedence).
   219→- Values are coerced to the field's native Python type (`int` for `status`, `float` for `response_time`, `str` for `host`/`method`/etc.).
   220→- `~` does case-insensitive substring match on string fields.
   221→

   223→
   224→### Tests (`tests/test_query.py`)

   231→| `test_filter_status_gte` | `"status >= 400"` returns 4xx/5xx only |
   232→| `test_filter_method` | `"method = GET"` returns only GETs |
   233→| `test_filter_combined_and` | `"status >= 400 AND method = POST"` ANDs correctly |
   234→| `test_filter_or` | `"status = 200 OR status = 404"` returns both |
   235→| `test_filter_response_time` | Float comparison works on `response_time` |
   236→| `test_filter_path_contains` | `"path ~ /api"` substring match |
   237→| `test_no_match` | Non-matching filter → empty output, exit 0 |
   238→| `test_stdin_query` | `source = -` works with filter |
   239→

   245→- **`project/ARCHITECTURE.md`**: New file — forward-looking query pipeline design (created alongside this plan).
   246→

   248→
   249→### What is NOT in this plan
   250→
   251→- WINDOW stage: time-based stream partitioning
   252→- AGGREGATE stage: `GROUP BY`, `COUNT`, `AVG`, `SUM`, `P95`, etc.
   253→- SELECT stage: field projection, computed columns
   254→- HAVING stage: post-aggregate filtering
   255→- Output formats (CSV, table)
   256→- Sorting / `LIMIT`
   257→
   258→See [ARCHITECTURE.md](../project/ARCHITECTURE.md) for how these fit into the full design.

Perfect! I now have a comprehensive understanding of the repository. Let me compile a thorough summary for you.

## Comprehensive Repository Overview

### 1. PROJECT OVERVIEW

**agentic-coding-talk** is a repository containing materials for a guest lecture on agentic coding for the University of Colorado's ATLAS program (ATLS 4214: Big Data Architecture). The repository demonstrates both the *concepts* of agentic coding and the *workflow* itself — it was built using the exact techniques being taught.

**Core Thesis**: Agentic coding tools don't change what good software looks like; they change *how fast* you can get there and where you spend your attention.

The project has two concurrent deliverables:
- **`slides/`** — Marp-based slide deck covering agentic coding concepts, workflows, and tooling
- **`project/`** — `logpipe`, a toy log analytics CLI tool used as a live demo, built using agentic coding techniques

---

### 2. DIRECTORY STRUCTURE & KEY FILES

```
agentic-coding-talk/
├── README.md                          # Top-level intro
├── AGENT.md                           # Context for AI coding agents working in this repo
├── slides/                            # Marp slide deck
│   ├── slides.md                      # Slide content (Markdown)
│   ├── slides.html                    # Exported HTML (self-contained)
│   ├── package.json                   # Node.js deps (@marp-team/marp-cli)
│   ├── package-lock.json
│   ├── README.md                      # Slides setup instructions
│   └── dist/                          # Built outputs (slides.html, slides.pdf)
├── project/                           # logpipe tool
│   ├── README.md                      # Setup and usage instructions
│   ├── ARCHITECTURE.md                # Forward-looking query pipeline design (7.5KB)
│   ├── pyproject.toml                 # Python project metadata
│   ├── uv.lock                        # Dependency lock file
│   ├── logpipe/                       # Main package
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── __main__.py
│   │   ├── cli.py                     # CLI commands (ingest, query)
│   │   ├── parser.py                  # Log parsing (regex → LogRecord dataclass)
│   │   └── query.py                   # Query pipeline: Stage, Pipeline, WhereStage, expression parsing (237 lines)
│   └── tests/                         # Black-box integration tests
│       ├── conftest.py                # Fixtures and CLI test harness (55 lines)
│       ├── test_ingest.py             # Tests for ingest command (52 lines)
│       └── test_query.py              # Tests for query/filtering (94 lines)
├── _plans/                            # Planning documents (showing top-down planning workflow)
│   ├── 2026-03-18-slides-skeleton-high-level.md

    └── settings.json, settings.local.json
```

---

### 3. THE LOGPIPE PROJECT

**What it is**: A CLI tool for ingesting and querying web server access logs (Extended Combined Log Format with appended response_time field).

**Current Implementation Status**:

| Feature | Status | Details |
|---------|--------|---------|
| **Parsing** | Complete | Regex-based log parsing into `LogRecord` dataclass |
| **Ingest command** | Complete | `logpipe ingest <file\|->`  — streams parsed logs as JSON |
| **Query/WHERE filtering** | Complete | `logpipe query "expr" <file\|->`  — filters by boolean predicates |
| **WINDOW (time bucketing)** | Planned | Future stage |
| **AGGREGATE (grouping)** | Planned | Future stage with COUNT, AVG, SUM, P95, etc. |
| **SELECT (projection)** | Planned | Future stage for field selection |
| **HAVING (post-agg filtering)** | Planned | Future stage |

**Command Examples**:
```bash
# Ingest: parse and echo as JSON
logpipe ingest access.log
logpipe ingest -  # read stdin

# Query: filter with WHERE expressions
logpipe query "status >= 400" access.log
logpipe query "status >= 400 AND method = POST" access.log
logpipe query "path ~ /api" access.log
logpipe query "response_time > 1.0" access.log
```

**Key Implementation Details**:

- **`parser.py`** (45 lines): Regex-based log parsing
  - `LogRecord` dataclass with 8 fields: host, user, ts (Unix epoch int), method, path, status, bytes, response_time
  - `parse_log_record()` returns `LogRecord | None`

- **`query.py`** (237 lines): Pipeline architecture with expression parsing
  - `Stage` protocol: stages are composable filters
  - `Pipeline` class: chains stages lazily
  - `WhereStage` dataclass: applies a predicate to filter records
  - `parse_predicate()`: hand-rolled tokenizer + recursive descent parser
  - Expression grammar supports: `=`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `~` operators with AND/OR combinators
  - Type-safe `Op = Literal[...]` type for operators

- **`cli.py`** (50 lines): Typer-based CLI with two commands
  - `ingest`: streams parsed logs as JSON lines
  - `query`: applies filter pipeline and outputs matching records

**Testing**: Black-box integration tests (no mocking)
- `test_ingest.py`: Basic parsing and echo functionality
- `test_query.py`: Filter correctness (status, method, path, response_time, substring matching, AND/OR logic)
- Tests invoke the actual CLI via subprocess against real log files

**Dependencies**:
- `typer` (CLI framework)
- `pytest` (dev dependency)
- `uv` (Python package manager)
- Requires Python 3.11+

**Architecture Document**: `/Users/eoin/workspace/agentic-coding-talk/project/ARCHITECTURE.md` (250 lines)
- Describes full SQL-style execution pipeline: `FROM → WHERE → WINDOW → AGGREGATE → SELECT → HAVING`

## What is Entire.io?

Entire is a **Git-native session recorder** for AI coding agents. It captures the full context of an agent session — conversation transcript, code changes, token usage, reasoning — and stores it permanently as versioned data in Git alongside your commits.

**Core data model:**

- **Session** — one complete agent interaction (start → finish). Nested sessions are captured when agents spawn sub-agents (e.g., Claude Code's Task tool). Contains transcript, tool calls, token usage, line attribution.
- **Checkpoint** — a snapshot tied to a Git commit. The checkpoint ID is embedded as a commit message trailer (`Entire-Checkpoint: a3b2c4d5e6f7`). Two types:
  - *Temporary* — live snapshots on shadow branches (`entire/<sessionID>-<worktreeID>`) during active sessions. Let you rewind mid-session.
  - *Committed* — permanent metadata stored on `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch and synced to GitHub.
- **Repository** — a connected GitHub repo with the Git hook installed.

Sessions and checkpoints have a flexible many-to-many relationship: one commit can span multiple agent sessions; one session can produce multiple commits.

---

## How you could use it better in this repo

This repo is literally a talk *about* agentic coding workflows, and Entire is already mentioned in the slides as a "bottom-up recording" tool. A few ideas:

1. **Capture the remaining logpipe sessions.** You have `_plans/` for top-down planning artifacts, but the Entire sessions for future stages (WINDOW, AGGREGATE, SELECT, HAVING) would give you live footage of the exact workflow you're teaching — real decisions, dead ends, and reasoning visible in the transcript.

2. **Use sessions as talk material.** The web app lets you browse transcripts with tool calls and diffs. You could pull up an actual session during the live demo to show students what "the agent's reasoning" looks like, rather than just narrating it.

3. **Highlight nested sessions.** Since Claude Code uses sub-agents (Task tool), Entire captures those as nested sessions. That directly illustrates multi-agent orchestration — a concrete artifact you could show in the talk.

4. **Make the workflow self-referential.** The talk's thesis is "show your work." Pushing the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch to GitHub means the session history is *in the repo* — reviewable, permanent, part of the story you're telling about how this repo was built.

5. **Token usage data.** Entire tracks per-checkpoint token costs. That's a concrete, real number you could reference in the slides when talking about the economics of agentic coding.

## What is Entire.io?

Entire is a **Git-native session recorder** for AI coding agents. It captures the full context of an agent session — conversation transcript, code changes, token usage, reasoning — and stores it permanently as versioned data in Git alongside your commits.

## Data Model

- **Session** — one complete agent interaction (start → finish). Nested sessions are captured when agents spawn sub-agents (e.g., Claude Code's Task tool). Contains transcript, tool calls, token usage, and line attribution.
- **Checkpoint** — a snapshot tied to a Git commit. The checkpoint ID is embedded as a commit message trailer (`Entire-Checkpoint: a3b2c4d5e6f7`). Two types:

  - *Committed* — permanent metadata stored on `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch and synced to GitHub.
- **Repository** — a connected GitHub repo with the Git hook installed.

Sessions and checkpoints have a flexible many-to-many relationship: one commit can span multiple agent sessions; one session can produce multiple commits.

## How Entire.io is Being Used in This Repo

## [​](#sessions)Sessions

A session represents a complete interaction with an AI coding agent from start to finish. When you start Claude Code, or another supported agent, Entire automatically creates a new session.

### [​](#what’s-captured-in-a-session)What’s Captured in a Session

- Conversation transcript - The prompts you sent and responses from the AI

- Code changes - All file modifications made during the session

- Checkpoints - Save points you can rewind to

- Token usage - Input/output tokens, cache usage, and API call counts

- Metadata - Timestamps, agent type, and configuration

- Line Attribution - A calculation of how many lines agents and humans contributed to the session

All sessions are stored locally in your project folder as well as on GitHub when you push. To find your raw session data on GitHub, check out the [Sessions Branch](#sessions-branch) section below.

### [​](#nested-sessions)Nested Sessions

When an AI agent spawns sub-agents (like Claude Code’s Task tool), Entire captures these as nested sessions within the parent session, preserving the full hierarchy of interactions.

## [​](#checkpoints)Checkpoints

A checkpoint is a snapshot that you can rewind to—think of it as a “save point” in your work.
Checkpoints have a 12-character hex ID, like: `8a513f56ed70`, You’ll find Checkpoint IDs in the web application as well as in the CLI.

### [​](#types-of-checkpoints)Types of Checkpoints

Temporary Checkpoints
Created on shadow branches during active sessions. These contain the full state snapshot
with code and metadata, allowing you to rewind at any point during your session.  Shadow branches
are named `entire/<sessionID>-<worktreeID>`, but remain local.
Committed Checkpoints
When you make a Git commit, the checkpoint metadata is stored permanently on the
`entire/checkpoints/v1` branch, which gets synced with your upstream. The checkpoint ID is added
to your commit message as a trailer:Report inc

## [​](#checkpoints-and-sessions)Checkpoints and Sessions

Checkpoints and sessions have a many-to-many relationship depending on the developer’s workflow. Here are the scenarios:

- Single checkpoint, single session - The most common workflow. You ask Claude Code to implement a feature, it does it and then you create a commit and push it.

- Single checkpoint, multiple sessions - A developer might have multiple Claude Code sessions working on a single feature and once done, the developer pushes a single checkpoint.

- Multiple checkpoints, single session - A developer might have a single session running in a terminal while creating multiple commits throughout that session and thereby creating multiple checkpoints. Entire will match up these checkpoints to the single session.

- Multiple checkpoints, multiple sessions - A developer might have multiple terminals with multiple sessions and checkpoints.

## [​](#viewing-sessions)Viewing Sessions

Click on a checkpoint to view it’s sessions. Each session card shows:

- Date and time - When the session occurred

- Duration - How long the session lasted

- Steps - How many steps the conversation took between the user and agent

- Tool Calls - How many tools were called

- Token usage - Total tokens consumed

Attribution - The percentage breakdown between AI Agents and Humans who contributed to the code
See a breakdown of who wrote the code: agent vs. human.

- Your prompts - What you asked the AI

- AI responses - What the agent replied

Conversation transcripts help during code review—reviewers can see the reasoning behind changes.

[Entire home page](/)Search...⌘KAsk AISearch...NavigationIntegrationsClaude Code[Documentation](/introduction)[Documentation](/introduction)Claude Code is Anthropic’s official CLI for Claude, designed for software engineering tasks. Entire has first-class integration with Claude Code, automatically capturing sessions and checkpoints.

## [​](#prerequisites)Prerequisites

- [Entire CLI installed](/cli/installation)

- [Claude Code installed](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code)

- A Git repository with Entire enabled

## [​](#how-it-works)How It Works

Entire automatically detects when Claude Code is active and begins capturing the session. Entire integrates with Claude Code via Git hooks:

- Session Detection - When Claude Code starts, Entire detects the process

- Hook Triggers - Git hooks fire on file changes and commits

- Checkpoint Creation - Checkpoints are created when you or the agent make a Git commit

- Metadata Capture - Session data is on the `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch

### [​](#captured-data)Captured Data

Entire captures the following from Claude Code sessions:
DataDescriptionConversationFull transcript of prompts and responsesFile changesAll files modified during the sessionTool callsCommands executed, files read, etc.Token usageInput, output, and cache tokensTimestampsStart time, checkpoints, end time

## [​](#features)Features

### [​](#nested-session-support)Nested Session Support

When Claude Code spawns sub-agents via the Task tool, Entire captures these as nested sessions:
Report incorrect codeCopyAsk AI
```
Main Session
├── Checkpoint 1
├── Sub-agent Session (Task: "Run tests")
│   ├── Sub-checkpoint 1
│   └── Sub-checkpoint 2
└── Checkpoint 2

```

Nested sessions preserve the full context of complex multi-agent workflows.

### [​](#rewind-during-sessions)Rewind During Sessions

Use `entire rewind` while Claude Code is running to restore to a previous checkpoint:
Report incorrect codeCopyAsk AI
```
# In another terminal - interactive mode
entire rewind

# Or rewind to a specific checkpoint
entire rewind --to checkpoint-id>

def consume(self, context: str = "token") -> str:
        if self.pos >= len(self.tokens):
            raise ValueError(
                f"Unexpected end of expression (expected {context}): {self.expr!r}"
            )
        tok = self.tokens[self.pos]
        self.pos += 1
        return tok

## Summary of Findings from Entire.io Documentation

Based on my search of the Entire.io documentation, here are the specific answers to your questions:

### 1. **How/when a new Session is created — Process-based or In-session commands?**

**Process-based creation:**
From the Core Concepts documentation:
> "A session represents a complete interaction with an AI coding agent from start to finish. When you start Claude Code, or another supported agent, Entire automatically creates a new session."

From the Claude Code Integration docs:
> "Entire automatically detects when Claude Code is active and begins capturing the session... Session Detection - When Claude Code starts, Entire detects the process"

**Answer:** Sessions are **process-based**. A new session is created when you start Claude Code (or another supported agent). It is not triggered by in-session commands.

---

### 2. **Whether there is any way to start a new session without restarting the agent process**

Based on the documentation reviewed, there is **no explicit mechanism** to start a new session without restarting the agent process. The documentation shows:
- Sessions are tied to process start events
- The `entire resume` command allows resuming from a previous branch/session, but doesn't create a new one
- The `entire reset` command (see below) terminates the current session state, but requires a fresh agent process to create a new session

---

### 3. **Any mention of Claude Code's /clear or /reset commands and how they interact with session tracking**

The Entire CLI has a **`reset` command** (not `/clear`):

From the CLI Commands documentation:
> "**reset** - Delete the shadow branch and session state for the current commit. Use this to start fresh."
> ```
> entire reset [flags]
> ```
> Flag: `--force` - Skip confirmation prompt
> 
> "This deletes session data for the current commit. The data cannot be recovered."

**Answer:** There is an `entire reset` command (not a Claude Code `/clear` or `/reset`). This command terminates the current session's shadow branch and session state, allowing you to "start fresh." However, this is a destructive operation that deletes session data and appears to be separate from Claude Code's own commands.

---

### 4. **How sessions end / what signals session boundaries**

**Session lifecycle phases:**
From the CLI Commands documentation (`doctor` command):
> "A session is considered stuck if:
> - It is in ACTIVE or ACTIVE_COMMITTED phase with no interaction for over 1 hour
> - It is in ENDED phase with uncondensed checkpoint data on a shadow branch"

**Session boundaries:**
Sessions have these lifecycle states:
- **ACTIVE** - Session is currently running
- **ACTIVE_COMMITTED** - Session has commits but is still running
- **ENDED** - Session has terminated

**Session data preservation:**
From the Core Concepts documentation:
> "All sessions are stored locally in your project folder as well as on GitHub when you push."

**Nested sessions:**
> "When an AI agent spawns sub-agents (like Claude Code's Task tool), Entire captures these as nested sessions within the parent session, preserving the full hierarchy of interactions."

**Answer:** Sessions end when the agent process terminates. Sessions transition from ACTIVE → ACTIVE_COMMITTED → ENDED. Sessions older than 1 hour without interaction are flagged as "stuck" and can be condensed (saved to permanent storage on `entire/checkpoints/v1` branch) or discarded via the `entire doctor` command.

---

### Additional Context: `resume` vs `reset` vs `rewind`

- **`entire resume`** - Switch to a previous branch and resume the agent session from its last commit
- **`entire reset`** - Delete the shadow branch and session state (destructive; starts fresh)
- **`entire rewind`** - Rewind to a previous checkpoint *within* the current session (non-destructive)

- **`/clear`** — clears conversation history but keeps the same process running. Entire would see this as the same session continuing.
- **`/reset`** — same situation; it's a Claude Code in-session command, not a process restart.

**To get a clean session boundary:** exit Claude Code (`/exit` or Ctrl+D) and start it again. That's what signals a new session to Entire.

There is an **`entire reset`** CLI command (separate from Claude's `/reset`) that deletes the shadow branch and session state for a fresh start — but it's destructive and unrecoverable, so it's more of an "undo a bad session" tool than a workflow boundary tool.

The practical pattern for clean visualization would be: commit your work unit, exit Claude Code, start a new session for the next unit. Since Entire already ties checkpoints to commits, your session-per-feature boundary naturally maps to commit-per-feature anyway.

content.text:
Almost certainly yes — the VS Code "new session" button starts a fresh Claude Code process, which is exactly what Entire detects as a session boundary. It's the same signal as exiting and restarting from the terminal.

I'm not 100% certain of the VS Code extension internals, but that's the expected behavior given Entire's process-based detection model.
